text:
ABBCcommentator was corrected by a colleague after misgendering American star Raven Saunders at theOlympics.

Steve Backley, who representedTeam GBin the javelin before moving into the media, described Saunders as ‘her’ during the women’s shot put qualifying event at the Stade de France.

Saunders, 28, identifies as non-binary and uses they-them pronouns, which was pointed out by BBC co-commentator Jazmin Sawyers.

Backley said it was ‘good to see’ the ‘colourful character’ of Saunders back in action, in reference to their distinctive look.

Saunders has oftencompeted wearing a mask that covers their entire face since Covid, partly as they say it helps them get in the zone and also because they got so used to wearing one duringthe pandemic.

They also dyed their hair green and purple for the Paris Games and had gold grills over their teeth.

‘The colorful character of Raven Saunders back,’ said Backley, a former world record javelin thrower who won three Olympic medals.

More gender madness at the Olympics in the womenâs shot put. The host introduces Raven Saunders, saying âitâs good to see her backâ.He is promptly corrected by his co-host, saying âthey are actually non-binaryâ.If theyâre not a woman, why are they in the womenâs competition?pic.twitter.com/i0FFHyAWKX

‘Good to see her back, sort of. Sort of see her, I mean.’

Great Britain long jumper Sawyers then said on BBC’s coverage: ‘Well we can’t see them very well.

‘Raven Saunders is actually non-binary and wearing the mask there, we’re quite used to seeing them with interesting attire.’

Saunders qualified for the women’s shot put final, which takes place on Friday, with a distance of 18.62m with their third throw.

They will look to go one better from Tokyo 2020 when they won silver medal in the shot put.

Explaining their decision to wear a mask while competing, Saunders said back in 2021: ‘Early on, similar to the Hulk, I had a tough time differentiating between the two; I had a tough time controlling when the Hulk came out or when the Hulk didn’t come out.

‘But through my journey, especially dealing with mental health and things like that, I learned how to compartmentalize, the same way that Bruce Banner learned to control the Hulk, learned how to let the Hulk come out during the right moments and that way it also gave him a sign of mental peace.

‘But when the Hulk came out, the Hulk was smashing everything that needed to be smashed.’

Saunders revealed they had been struggling mentally ahead of the Paris Games but hoped to inspire others.

‘I honestly said that If I made this team, when I made this team, that it was for the people,’ Saunders said.

‘It was for the people that reminded me of who I was when I was down, when I was out, when I was suspended.’

Asked if they were planning to change their look for Friday’s final, Saunders added: ‘I have something even better.’

title: BBC commentator corrected after misgendering US Olympics star | Metro News
description: A BBC Olympics commentator was corrected after misgendering American shot put star Raven Saunders during the Paris 2024 Games.
